title: The boring half of growth: why most experiments don't ship
summary: On the unglamorous loop between an idea and a working experiment, and why most teams never close it.
body: Most teams I've worked with don't have an ideas problem. They have a closure problem. The doc gets written, the slack thread gets long, the dashboard gets opened — and then nothing actually changes in the product.

Growth is two halves. The first half is glamorous: spotting a friction, sketching a hypothesis, arguing about whether copy or layout matters more. The second half is the boring one — instrumenting the page, checking the funnel, fighting with QA, writing the experiment doc, deciding what 'won' actually means.
If you skip the boring half, you don't have a growth program. You have a list of opinions with a Notion link.
The fix isn't a better framework. It's a posture: treat shipping the measurement as part of shipping the experiment. Done means decided.

title: Latency is a product surface
summary: Notes on how response time shapes trust, monetisation, and the felt sense of intelligence in AI products.
body: We talk about latency like it's a performance number — p95, p99, time-to-first-token. But in AI products, latency is a felt surface. It's the moment where the user decides whether the product is thinking, stalling, or broken.
This piece is still being written. The shape of it: latency as ad inventory, latency as trust, and the small UX decisions that turn waiting into anticipation.

title: Marketplaces win on measurement, not supply
summary: Why GPS, attribution, and unit economics matter more than another driver acquisition campaign.
body: Every two-sided marketplace I've sketched ends up with the same realisation: the bottleneck isn't acquiring supply or demand. It's that nobody trusts the numbers.
More to come — this one is brewing alongside the Moving Targets case study.

title: Good products feel obvious only after they're built
summary: A short essay on taste, ambiguity, and the quiet work of deciding what deserves to exist.
body: Type 'product' on this site for the short version. The long version is still being written.

title: 0 → 1 isn't a phase, it's a posture
summary: What changes (and what doesn't) when you stop optimising and start originating.
body: Most of us are taught to optimise. The hardest part of 0 → 1 is unlearning the gravitational pull of the existing graph.
